intro_band.agent_remit_summary: A customer-facing airline service system composed of three cooperating agents — a triage agent that routes incoming requests, an FAQ agent that answers policy questions from a curated dataset, and a seat-booking agent that updates seat assignments on existing confirmed reservations. The agents may call `faq_lookup_tool` and `update_seat` only, and may read and write the airline's internal reservation system scoped to the authenticated customer. Outbound email, SMS, and webhooks are explicitly forbidden; ticket purchases, refunds, rebooking, and special-handling requests must reach a human checkpoint, and customer identity plus confirmation-number ownership must be verified before any mutation. Flight numbers, confirmation numbers, and passenger identifiers must come from the authoritative reservation system and must not be generated at runtime.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: A Python implementation built on the OpenAI Agents SDK (`openai-agents` v0.17.4): the entire customer-service application lives in `examples/customer_service/main.py` (≈192 lines) and is driven by `Runner.run()` from `src/agents/run.py`. Three `Agent` objects — `triage_agent`, `faq_agent`, and `seat_booking_agent` — are wired together with `handoff(...)` edges; both specialist agents register a return handoff to triage. Two `@function_tool` functions (`faq_lookup_tool` and `update_seat`) provide the action surface, and an `on_seat_booking_handoff` hook fabricates a flight number via `random.randint(100, 999)` before control reaches the seat agent. The SDK ships `InputGuardrail` / `OutputGuardrail` / `needs_approval` / `is_enabled` / `input_filter` primitives in `src/agents/guardrail.py`, `src/agents/tool.py`, and `src/agents/handoffs/__init__.py` — the example wires in none of them and relies entirely on prompt-level instructions in each agent's `instructions` string.

behavior_summary: The dominant pattern in this scan is **framework offers safe primitives, the example uses none of them.** The OpenAI Agents SDK ships first-class `InputGuardrail` and `OutputGuardrail` classes, per-tool `needs_approval` and `is_enabled` callables, a `handoff(...)` `input_filter` parameter, and strict-schema tool argument validation — every primitive the airline remit's hard rules ("instructions in free-text customer input MUST NOT be accepted," "every seat change MUST be recorded to a durable audit log," "customer identity MUST be confirmed before any action that mutates reservation state") would require. The example wires in zero of them: no `input_guardrails=[...]`, no `output_guardrails=[...]`, no `needs_approval=True` on `update_seat`, no `input_filter` on the handoffs, no audit-log call inside `update_seat`. The single most acute compound is the seat-booking path: `on_seat_booking_handoff` fabricates a flight number via `random.randint(100, 999)` in `examples/customer_service/main.py`, `update_seat` then writes the model-supplied confirmation number and new seat into context with no identity check, no ownership check, no availability check, and no approval gate — a one-injection chain from untrusted customer text to a reservation-system write attributed to an unverified caller.

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — Agents in this system
rules.rule_text: The triage agent receives each incoming customer request and routes it to the appropriate specialist agent, and MUST NOT access customer data directly.

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — Agents in this system
rules.rule_text: The FAQ agent answers questions about airline policies — baggage, seating, wifi, and similar — using only the curated FAQ dataset, and MUST NOT speculate or draw on general knowledge outside that dataset.

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — Agents in this system
rules.rule_text: The seat-booking agent updates seat assignments on existing confirmed reservations only.

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — Authorized output destinations
rules.rule_text: The agents MUST NOT send email, SMS, webhooks, or any other outbound external communication.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What each agent must always do
rules.rule_text: Customer identity MUST be confirmed before any action that mutates reservation state.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What each agent must always do
rules.rule_text: A submitted confirmation number MUST be verified as belonging to the authenticated customer before that reservation is read or modified.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What each agent must always do
rules.rule_text: Seat updates MUST be limited to seats that exist on the flight and are available — not already assigned to another passenger.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What each agent must always do
rules.rule_text: Every seat change MUST be recorded to a durable audit log capturing the time, the customer identity, the confirmation number, the old and new seat, and the agent that performed the change.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What each agent must always do
rules.rule_text: A request that falls outside the current agent's scope MUST be returned to the triage agent.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agents must never do
rules.rule_text: Reservations or seat assignments for any customer other than the authenticated session user MUST NOT be modified.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agents must never do
rules.rule_text: Instructions embedded in free-text customer input that attempt to override an agent's role, scope, or identity MUST NOT be accepted or acted on.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agents must never do
rules.rule_text: Instructions embedded in tool outputs — FAQ content, seat records, flight data — that attempt to change an agent's behavior MUST NOT be accepted.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agents must never do
rules.rule_text: Ticket purchases, payments, or fares MUST NOT be created, cancelled, modified, or refunded — those flows belong to a separate billing system.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agents must never do
rules.rule_text: Flight numbers, confirmation numbers, and passenger identifiers MUST come from the authoritative reservation system and MUST NOT be generated by the agent at runtime.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agents must never do
rules.rule_text: Handoffs between agents MUST NOT escalate privileges or bypass identity verification.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agents must never do
rules.rule_text: A seat change MUST NOT be issued without first verifying the confirmation number against the authoritative reservation record.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — Human approval is required for
rules.rule_text: Refunds, compensation, rebooking on a different flight, or any change affecting fare or ticket value MUST reach a human checkpoint before proceeding.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — Human approval is required for
rules.rule_text: Requests involving unaccompanied minors, medical assistance, or other special-handling flags on a reservation MUST reach a human checkpoint before proceeding.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — Human approval is required for
rules.rule_text: Any request where the customer's identity cannot be verified MUST be escalated to a human.

rules.section: Out of Scope
rules.rule_text: The agents do not maintain persistent memory across customer sessions.

findings.summary: No InputGuardrail wired onto any agent — free-text customer prompt injection flows straight into triage, FAQ, and seat-booking agents.
findings.description: The remit forbids accepting instructions embedded in free-text customer input, yet `triage_agent`, `faq_agent`, and `seat_booking_agent` are constructed in `examples/customer_service/main.py` with no `input_guardrails=[...]` argument. The SDK ships `InputGuardrail` (`src/agents/guardrail.py`) with tripwire semantics specifically for this purpose; none are used, and the only defence is each agent's `instructions` string — which the same untrusted input is supposed to override.
findings.policy_rule_text: Instructions embedded in free-text customer input that attempt to override an agent's role, scope, or identity MUST NOT be accepted or acted on.

evidence.snippet: faq_agent, seat_booking_agent, and triage_agent constructors (lines 96-138) — none pass input_guardrails=[...]
InputGuardrail dataclass exists with tripwire_triggered semantics — the example simply does not wire any in

recommended_actions: Define an `InputGuardrail` that scans the latest user turn for instruction-override patterns (role-redefinition, "ignore previous", out-of-scope keywords) and trips the tripwire on match; attach it to all three agents via `input_guardrails=[...]`.
Add a complementary guardrail that runs only on `triage_agent` to reject requests in the remit's "does NOT do" list (refund, cancel, rebook, upgrade, special-handling) before any handoff fires.

findings.summary: update_seat writes a model-supplied confirmation number into context with no reservation-system verification and no approval gate.
findings.description: The remit requires that a seat change MUST NOT be issued without first verifying the confirmation number against the authoritative reservation record, but `update_seat` (`examples/customer_service/main.py:67-83`) takes `confirmation_number` and `new_seat` as raw string arguments from the model and assigns them straight to `context.context` with no lookup, no ownership check, and no approval prompt. The SDK supports `needs_approval: bool | Callable` on `FunctionTool` (`src/agents/tool.py:328`) — the decorator is not configured to use it.
findings.policy_rule_text: The seat-booking agent updates seat assignments on existing confirmed reservations only. / A seat change MUST NOT be issued without first verifying the confirmation number against the authoritative reservation record.

evidence.snippet: update_seat function — directly assigns context.context.confirmation_number = confirmation_number and context.context.seat_number = new_seat with no verification, no audit log, no approval
FunctionTool exposes needs_approval (bool or Callable) for exactly this case; @function_tool on update_seat does not set it

recommended_actions: Set `needs_approval=True` on `@function_tool` for `update_seat` (or a callable that requires approval whenever the caller's confirmation number does not already match the authenticated session) so the run pauses for operator review before the write lands.

Add a reservation-system lookup inside `update_seat` that resolves `confirmation_number` against the authenticated customer and short-circuits with an error before touching context if ownership cannot be verified.

findings.summary: No identity verification, no confirmation-number ownership check, and no human escalation when identity cannot be established — the seat path runs entirely on model-trusted strings.
findings.description: The remit's hard rules require that customer identity MUST be confirmed before any mutation, that a submitted confirmation number MUST be verified as belonging to the authenticated customer, that reservations for other customers MUST NOT be modified, that handoffs MUST NOT bypass identity verification, and that any unverifiable identity MUST be escalated to a human. None of these are enforced in code. `AirlineAgentContext` (`examples/customer_service/main.py:29-33`) carries `passenger_name`, `confirmation_number`, and `seat_number` as plain optional strings populated by the model from chat input; no authenticated session principal exists. `update_seat` does no lookup against any reservation system and no ownership check before writing. The triage→seat handoff calls `on_seat_booking_handoff` which mutates context without any identity gate.
findings.policy_rule_text: Customer identity MUST be confirmed before any action that mutates reservation state. / A submitted confirmation number MUST be verified as belonging to the authenticated customer before that reservation is read or modified. / Reservations or seat assignments for any customer other than the authenticated session user MUST NOT be modified. / Handoffs between agents MUST NOT escalate privileges or bypass identity verification. / Any request where the customer's identity cannot be verified MUST be escalated to a human.

evidence.snippet: AirlineAgentContext fields (passenger_name, confirmation_number, seat_number, flight_number) — all populated from model-mediated chat with no authenticated principal anywhere in the data model
on_seat_booking_handoff(context) — fires on every triage→seat handoff with zero identity check, zero ownership verification, zero human-escalation branch

update_seat assigns context.context.confirmation_number = confirmation_number directly — accepts any confirmation number the model passes without ever asking "does this belong to the authenticated caller?"

recommended_actions: Add an authenticated session principal to `AirlineAgentContext` (e.g. a `session_user_id` set by the application layer before `Runner.run` is called) and gate every mutation on `confirmation_number` belonging to that principal.

In `on_seat_booking_handoff`, refuse to proceed (raise or set a "must-escalate" flag) when no authenticated session is attached to the context, so a triage→seat handoff cannot fire for an anonymous caller.
Wire an `InputGuardrail` on `triage_agent` that escalates to a human (returns a tripwire) whenever the context lacks a verified identity and the request is anything other than an FAQ.

findings.summary: Flight number is fabricated at runtime via random.randint(100, 999) inside on_seat_booking_handoff — the remit forbids agent-generated identifiers.
findings.description: The remit's "MUST NOT" list explicitly states that flight numbers, confirmation numbers, and passenger identifiers MUST come from the authoritative reservation system and MUST NOT be generated by the agent at runtime. `on_seat_booking_handoff` (`examples/customer_service/main.py:89-91`) does exactly the forbidden thing: it writes `flight_number = f"FLT-{random.randint(100, 999)}"` to context every time triage hands off to the seat agent. `update_seat` then asserts `context.context.flight_number is not None` and proceeds — the fabricated value is treated as load-bearing. There is no consultation of any flight catalog and no error path when the customer's actual flight cannot be resolved.
findings.policy_rule_text: Flight numbers, confirmation numbers, and passenger identifiers MUST come from the authoritative reservation system and MUST NOT be generated by the agent at runtime.

evidence.snippet: on_seat_booking_handoff — flight_number = f"FLT-{random.randint(100, 999)}"; context.context.flight_number = flight_number
update_seat asserts context.context.flight_number is not None — treats the random value as if it were authoritative reservation data

recommended_actions: Delete the `random.randint` fabrication entirely. Replace `on_seat_booking_handoff` with a lookup that resolves the customer's actual flight from the authoritative reservation system using the authenticated session identity, and fail the handoff (escalate to human) when no flight can be resolved.
Add a regression test that exercises `on_seat_booking_handoff` and asserts `random` is not in its call chain, so the fabrication cannot silently return in a future refactor.

findings.summary: No durable audit log of seat changes — the remit explicitly requires capturing time, customer, confirmation number, old/new seat, and acting agent for every change.
findings.description: The remit states that every seat change MUST be recorded to a durable audit log capturing time, customer identity, confirmation number, old and new seat, and the agent that performed the change. `update_seat` (`examples/customer_service/main.py:67-83`) writes the new seat into context and returns a confirmation string to the model — no logger call, no append to a persistent store, no domain audit event. Default SDK tracing (`src/agents/tracing/setup.py`) exports spans to OpenAI's tracing API but that is observability for the SDK consumer, not a durable airline-side audit log; OpenAI tracing is operationally unsuitable as the system-of-record for reservation mutations and does not capture old vs new seat as semantic fields.
findings.policy_rule_text: Every seat change MUST be recorded to a durable audit log capturing the time, the customer identity, the confirmation number, the old and new seat, and the agent that performed the change.

evidence.snippet: update_seat function body — only mutations are to context.context fields; no audit_log.append, no logger.info, no database INSERT, no event emission
Default trace processor exports to OpenAI's tracing backend — observability, not a durable domain audit trail the airline owns

recommended_actions: Inject a domain audit-log writer into `AirlineAgentContext` (e.g. `audit_log: AuditLogger`) and call `audit_log.record_seat_change(time, customer_id, confirmation_number, old_seat, new_seat, acting_agent="seat_booking_agent")` inside `update_seat` before returning success.
Make `update_seat` read the old seat from the reservation system before overwriting so the audit record captures both before and after states.

findings.summary: update_seat accepts any string as new_seat — no existence check, no availability check, no collision check with other passengers.
findings.description: The remit requires that seat updates MUST be limited to seats that exist on the flight and are available, not already assigned to another passenger. `update_seat` (`examples/customer_service/main.py:67-83`) takes `new_seat: str` straight from the model output, performs no parse against a valid seat-map for the flight, performs no "is this seat already taken" lookup, and writes whatever string the model returned into context. The model could pass `"99Z"`, `"crew jumpseat"`, or any other passenger's seat assignment, and the function returns success.
findings.policy_rule_text: Seat updates MUST be limited to seats that exist on the flight and are available — not already assigned to another passenger.

evidence.snippet: update_seat(context, confirmation_number: str, new_seat: str) — new_seat is a free string with no validator, no enum, no seat-map consultation
context.context.seat_number = new_seat — write occurs unconditionally; no collision check against any other passenger's assignment

recommended_actions: Replace the free-string `new_seat` parameter with a validator that resolves the seat against the flight's actual seat map (existence) and current assignments (availability), rejecting writes that would overwrite another passenger.
Add a `tool_input_guardrails` entry on `update_seat`'s `FunctionTool` that short-circuits when `new_seat` does not match the flight's seat-map regex before the function body runs.

findings.summary: Triage agent has no code-level restriction on data access — the "MUST NOT access customer data directly" rule is prompt-only.
findings.description: The remit says the triage agent receives requests and routes them, and MUST NOT access customer data directly. In code, `triage_agent` (`examples/customer_service/main.py:123-138`) has no tools other than its two handoffs — so it currently *cannot* call `faq_lookup_tool` or `update_seat` directly, which is a structural positive. But the restriction is enforced only by the empty `tools=[]` default, not by any explicit allowlist, and the agent's `instructions` string does not state the prohibition. A future refactor that adds a "convenience" tool to triage (e.g. a "quick lookup") would silently violate the remit with no test catching it.
findings.policy_rule_text: The triage agent receives each incoming customer request and routes it to the appropriate specialist agent, and MUST NOT access customer data directly.

evidence.snippet: triage_agent constructor — no tools= argument (so tools defaults to empty), but no explicit assertion or test that the agent MUST stay tool-less

recommended_actions: Add an explicit `tools=[]` to `triage_agent`'s constructor with a comment stating the remit prohibition, so the intent is visible to future maintainers.
Add a regression test that asserts `triage_agent.tools == []` and that triage's only available actions are the two handoff edges.

findings.summary: Forbidden flows (refund, rebook, special-handling, unverifiable identity) have no human-checkpoint mechanism — no needs_approval gate exists anywhere in the example.
findings.description: The remit's "Human approval is required for" section names refunds, compensation, rebooking, fare changes, unaccompanied-minor/medical/special-handling flags, and any unverifiable identity. None of the example's three agents expose a human-checkpoint hook. The `FunctionTool` class (`src/agents/tool.py:328`) supports `needs_approval: bool | Callable` for exactly this — pause the run for operator review — and the SDK's `RunState.approve()` / `RunState.reject()` machinery exists in `src/agents/run_state.py`. The example wires none of it. The seat agent's prompt says "transfer back to triage" for off-routine asks, but the prompt is the only enforcement, and triage has no escalation tool — meaning a refund request bounces between agents until the model decides to free-text deny it.
findings.policy_rule_text: A request that falls outside the current agent's scope MUST be returned to the triage agent. / Ticket purchases, payments, or fares MUST NOT be created, cancelled, modified, or refunded — those flows belong to a separate billing system. / Refunds, compensation, rebooking on a different flight, or any change affecting fare or ticket value MUST reach a human checkpoint before proceeding. / Requests involving unaccompanied minors, medical assistance, or other special-handling flags on a reservation MUST reach a human checkpoint before proceeding.

evidence.snippet: seat_booking_agent prompt says "transfer back to triage" for off-routine — prompt-level only, with no needs_approval tool and no human-escalation handoff target
FunctionTool.needs_approval (bool | Callable) — the SDK ships the primitive; the example uses it on zero tools

recommended_actions: Add a `request_human_review` tool to `triage_agent` with `needs_approval=True` and route every refund/rebook/special-handling intent to it via an `InputGuardrail` classifier.
Add a `must_escalate_for_unverified_identity` callable as `needs_approval` on `update_seat` so any write attempted without an authenticated session pauses for operator review.

findings.summary: No OutputGuardrail wired on any agent — instructions embedded in tool outputs flow back into the model with no inspection.
findings.description: The remit forbids accepting instructions embedded in tool outputs (FAQ content, seat records, flight data) that attempt to change an agent's behavior. The SDK ships `OutputGuardrail` in `src/agents/guardrail.py` for inspecting agent output and tripwiring when tampered. The example wires zero output guardrails — neither on the FAQ agent (whose `faq_lookup_tool` returns static strings today, but the surface is intended to evolve into a real FAQ database) nor on the seat agent (where `update_seat`'s return string is rendered into the model's context for the next turn). A future FAQ source that allows operator-authored or scraped content would let an injection embedded in an article body redirect the agent with no detection.
findings.policy_rule_text: Instructions embedded in tool outputs — FAQ content, seat records, flight data — that attempt to change an agent's behavior MUST NOT be accepted.

evidence.snippet: faq_agent and seat_booking_agent constructors — neither passes output_guardrails=[...]
OutputGuardrail dataclass and InputGuardrail dataclass both exist with tripwire semantics — neither is wired into the example

recommended_actions: Define an `OutputGuardrail` that scans the latest tool output and the agent's draft response for instruction-injection patterns and trips on match; attach it to `faq_agent` and `seat_booking_agent` via `output_guardrails=[...]`.

Add `tool_output_guardrails` on `update_seat` and `faq_lookup_tool` that strip or refuse outputs containing imperative second-person instructions or role-redefinition strings before they reach the model.

findings.summary: FAQ "MUST NOT speculate" enforced only by prompt — the FAQ agent retains general-knowledge capability and can answer outside the curated dataset.
findings.description: The remit says the FAQ agent answers questions about airline policies using only the curated FAQ dataset and MUST NOT speculate or draw on general knowledge. `faq_agent`'s `instructions` (`examples/customer_service/main.py:99-105`) ask the model to "Use the faq lookup tool to answer the question. Do not rely on your own knowledge" — but the underlying LLM still has all of its general knowledge available, the agent can still produce a `MessageOutputItem` directly without calling `faq_lookup_tool`, and no `OutputGuardrail` checks that the answer was sourced from the tool. The tool itself returns `"I'm sorry, I don't know..."` for unmatched queries (a real positive), but nothing stops the model from ignoring that and answering from its priors.
findings.policy_rule_text: The FAQ agent answers questions about airline policies — baggage, seating, wifi, and similar — using only the curated FAQ dataset, and MUST NOT speculate or draw on general knowledge outside that dataset.

evidence.snippet: faq_agent.instructions — "Do not rely on your own knowledge" is prompt-level guidance, not a code constraint
faq_lookup_tool returns "I'm sorry, I don't know the answer to that question." for unmatched queries — a positive for the tool itself, but the agent can still answer without calling it

recommended_actions: Add an `OutputGuardrail` on `faq_agent` that trips when the agent produces a substantive answer without a preceding `ToolCallItem` for `faq_lookup_tool` in the same turn, forcing tool-grounded replies.
Consider a tool-call-required mode for `faq_agent` (e.g. `model_settings=ModelSettings(tool_choice="required")`) so the model cannot answer without invoking the curated tool.

positives.title: Strict JSON-schema tool arguments by default
positives.description: `FunctionTool` defaults `strict_json_schema=True` and passes the tool schema through `ensure_strict_json_schema` at construction (`src/agents/tool.py:310,403`), so `faq_lookup_tool` and `update_seat` cannot be invoked with structurally malformed arguments — a real, operative defence against a class of model-output-as-injection attacks.

positives.title: Curated, deterministic FAQ lookup with explicit "don't know" path
positives.description: `faq_lookup_tool` is a keyword switch over a fixed dataset that returns a closed answer or `"I'm sorry, I don't know the answer to that question."` (`examples/customer_service/main.py:42-64`); the FAQ surface is genuinely scoped, in line with the remit's "MUST NOT speculate" clause for the FAQ agent.

positives.title: Dependencies upper-bounded and lockfile committed
positives.description: `pyproject.toml` ceilings every direct dependency (e.g. `openai>=2.36.0,<3`, `pydantic>=2.12.2,<3`, `mcp>=1.19.0,<2`) and the workspace ships `uv.lock` (`pyproject.toml:9-18`, `uv.lock`); a major-version surprise from a transitive update is bounded.

positives.title: Default tracing wired into every turn
positives.description: The SDK installs `BatchTraceProcessor` + `BackendSpanExporter` by default (`src/agents/tracing/setup.py:39`, `src/agents/tracing/processors.py:707`) and `main.py` wraps each loop iteration in `with trace("Customer service", group_id=conversation_id)` — every agent turn produces an exported trace span out of the box.

rows.path: OpenAI tracing API (BackendSpanExporter, https://api.openai.com tracing ingest)
rows.content_type: structured trace spans (Agents SDK trace + span schema)
rows.purpose: Captures one trace per session and per-agent spans for every turn — generation, tool calls, handoffs — exported to OpenAI's tracing backend

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Ad hoc. The agent inherits the SDK's strict-schema tool-argument validation and default tracing (genuine but minimal contributions to Zero Trust and Monitor Continuously), and rides on bounded, lockfile-pinned dependencies (a real supply-chain positive at the framework layer). Everything else the remit demands — input guardrails on customer text, output guardrails on tool returns, identity verification before mutation, durable audit logging of seat changes, approval gates on consequential writes, and any form of adversarial testing — is absent at the example's code level, so the customer-facing surface relies on prompt instructions the model can be talked out of. The weighted overall reflects an agent that is *demonstrating* an SDK rather than *deploying* its security primitives.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: Each agent's `instructions` string declares its scope ("answer FAQs," "update seats") and the triage agent has narrow handoffs, but the restriction is prompt-only — no code gate prevents the FAQ agent from answering off-topic, and the seat agent has no check that the request actually concerns a seat change rather than a refund or rebooking the remit forbids.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: `faq_lookup_tool` is a closed, curated keyword lookup with an "I don't know" fallback (a real positive for the FAQ flow), but customer free-text flows directly into every agent's LLM context with no validation, and `update_seat` accepts a model-supplied `confirmation_number` and `new_seat` as authoritative without consulting any reservation-system source of truth.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: Zero `InputGuardrail` or `OutputGuardrail` instances are wired onto any agent in `examples/customer_service/main.py`; `update_seat` has no `needs_approval`, performs no identity or ownership verification, and writes the model-supplied seat and confirmation number into context unconditionally; the only trust controls are prompt-level "routine" steps the model can be talked out of.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: `pyproject.toml` floors and ceilings every direct dependency (e.g. `openai>=2.36.0,<3`, `pydantic>=2.12.2,<3`, `mcp>=1.19.0,<2`) and the workspace ships a committed `uv.lock`, so a transient version-swap is bounded — but the example uses no model-version pinning, no ML-BOM, and the SDK itself is the trusted runtime with no integrity check at install time.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: The SDK's `tests/` directory contains `test_guardrails.py`, `test_tool_guardrails.py`, and `test_stream_input_guardrail_timing.py` — framework-level fixtures that exercise guardrail mechanics — but `examples/customer_service/` ships only `main.py` with no tests, no injection fixtures, no postmortems, and no evidence the example was probed against the airline remit's prohibitions before being shipped.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: The SDK installs a default `BatchTraceProcessor` + `BackendSpanExporter` that ships traces to OpenAI's tracing API (`src/agents/tracing/setup.py`, `src/agents/tracing/processors.py`) and the example wraps each turn in `with trace("Customer service", ...)`, so the agent is *traceable* — but there is no domain audit log of seat changes (the remit explicitly requires one), no logging of who attempted what mutation, and traces are observability for OpenAI, not an operational audit trail the airline can rely on.
